To advertise is to make actions that may be disadvantageous in the moment but serve to give the opponent a false impression about the advertising player's style, thereby luring him into making mistakes in the future.
body.paragraph2: This could mean that a player shows his cards after one or more bluffs, or lets himself be "caught" bluffing, so that his opponents think that he bluffs often, allowing him to cash in on good hands later

To go all-in is to bet all the chips one has at the table. Once a player is all-in, he is treated specially since he can no longer make any more wagers or call any more bets. He cannot be forced to fold and will see the showdown for sure. The players in the game who are still active finish playing the round normally.

shove, push

The ante is a form of forced bet that all players must pay before the cards are dealt. They are paid by all players at the table every round and do not count as bets made, but are a form of entry fee into the round.
body.paragraph2: This is in contrast to the blinds, which are paid by two different players every round. Seven Card Stud and Five Card Draw often use antes, though the question of antes vs. blinds is ultimately determined by the house rules.
body.paragraph3: In tournaments with blinds, it is usual to add antes after a particular blind level that every player must pay regardless of whether he paid a blind or not.

A backdoor draw or runner-runner draw is one that needs two instead of one card to complete. If a player has three clubs, for example, then he has a backdoor flush draw in clubs. He needs two more clubs for the flush.
The player has a backdoor flush draw in clubs and a backdoor straight draw. If the turn is a club, then he will have a regular flush draw. If it is a 4, 6, or 9, he will have a straight draw.
body.paragraph2: Backdoor draws are not very strong, but can be helpful in close situations. The chances of getting a flush with three cards of one suit are around 4.2 percent. In the example above, the player has around an 8.5% chance of getting a six and therefore, and open ended straight draw on the turn, and a 17% chance of obtaining an inside straight draw with a 4 or 9 that could be completed with a 6. All in all, he has a 3% chance of hitting a straight by the river.

A bad beat is a hand in which a player whose hand was highly favoured is beaten in an improbable turn of events. The boundary between a regular beat and a bad one varies from player to player. It's is generally thought that a winning hand whose chances of winning were around 5-10% is a bad beat, or if a player wins by hitting two perfect cards.

For advanced poker players, who are often up against other advanced players, it is important to balance their range. Balancing your range is a process where you manipulate the frequency with which different hands occur in the range. The goal here is to stay unpredictable for your opponents. This means that there may not be any hands in the range that occur much more frequently than others.
body.paragraph2: Back in the day, online poker players would only re-raise very strong hands, such as QQ, KK, AA and AK. Since this is a very small range, strong players could perfectly adapt their game and fold other, medium-strength hands. Over the course of time, some players started 3-betting a wider range of hands in order to exploit their opponents’ weak folds with hands such as JJ or AQ. From that point on, a 3-bet was no longer a reliable indicator of a big pair; players would now even re-raise hands such as 56s to expand and therefore balance their range. This in turn forced the opponents to adjust their ranges as well. They now had to play more than just their premium hands, as otherwise they would have folded the best hands too often.
body.paragraph3: Good players always try to put their opponents on a range. That's why it is also important postflop to balance your range in order to remain impossible to read. If you could be holding a variety of possible hands in a given situation and your opponent can't make any accurate assumptions about them, you have successfully balanced your range.

body.paragraph1: A player's bankroll is all the money that he uses for playing poker. It is the sum of funds that he has designated for gambling. In online poker, it denotes the money that a player has in his account.
Bankroll Management, BRM

Bankroll Management (BRM) is a system used by a player to manage his available bankroll, determining the level at which he participates in the game, thereby minimizing the risk of ruin due to the variance in wins and losses.
body.paragraph2: Conservative bankroll management means that a player does not move up to the next level until they have 30 times the full stack or 300 big blinds, and drops down to a qualifying level when there is not enough bankroll at a certain level.
body.paragraph3: Aggressive bankroll management may only require 10x the stack. Common bankroll rules are that you can only play at this level if you have 20-30 times your full stack (no limit or pot limit) or 200-300 big bets (limit).

A bet is one possible action a player can take if there are no bets on the table. To bet is to make a wager. A player can only bet if nobody else has bet. If there is already a wager on the table, then he needs to match that existing bet, although he can raise the wager if he wishes.
Check, Call, Fold, Raise, Overbet, All-In, Bet in the Dark

To bet in the dark is to make a wager on a future betting round before the cards for that round have been dealt. The player declares that he will bet, no matter what the cards do. For example, if a player in Texas Hold'em announces that he will bet on the flop in the dark, he means that he will make a bet regardless of what cards come up on the flop.
Bet, Check in the Dark

A betting pattern can be a player's betting habits in different situations. A betting pattern might be our conclusion about how a player plays a certain kind of hand, or inversely; observing a betting pattern can tell us something about what hand a player has. Advanced players vary their betting habits so that they do not give away information in this fashion.
Tell, Read, Line
Pattern

The betting structure refers to the rules that determine how wagers are made. It determines the maximum and minimum bet sizes, the number of raises allowed per round, the kinds of forced bets and all other related rules.
Bet, Raise, Cap, Fixed, Limit Pot, Limit, No-Limit

The big bet is the larger of two bet sizes in a game with a fixed bet size. In a $1/$2 Hold'em game, the big bet size is two dollars. The big bet is used in the later betting rounds. In Hold'em, for instance, players make small bets in the first two rounds and big bets thereafter.
Fixed Limit, Betting Structure, Small Bet
BB

The big blind is the larger of the two forced bets in a game with blinds. The smaller of the two is called the small blind. The blinds are paid before the cards are dealt, always by different players from round to round. The player to the left of the dealer pays the small blind and the player to his left pays the big blind.
Small Blind, Blind, Dealer

A blank or a brick is a board card that probably isn't of any help in completing any hand draw. If we say the turn is blank, we mean that the turn card did not change the game situation. For example, a turn card of 2 after a flop of three high (unsuited cards) would be described as blank.
Turn, Draw
Brick

Blinds are a form of forced bets in a game of poker. They are bets that must be paid before the cards are dealt. They are paid by two players on every round and these players change from round to round. The two bets may be the same size or one may be bigger than the other; there may be a big blind and a small blind.
body.paragraph2: The position of the dealer determines who pays the blinds. The two players immediately to the left of the dealer pay the blinds. As they count as bets for these two players, they don't have to match their own blinds again.
body.paragraph3: Antes are counterparts to blinds. They must be paid by every player at a table. Poker variants with blinds are Texas Hold'em, Omaha, and 2-7 Triple Draw, though the question of antes vs. blinds always depends on house rules.

A strategic counterpart of the blindsteal, the blind defense is an attempt to prevent the blinds from being stolen, often with a weak hand. The defense takes the form of a raise in oder to take the initiative or to force the attacker to fold or call. The plan is to then outplay the attacker in a later round, given the proper cards.
Blinds, Blindsteal

In a game with blinds as forced bets like Texas Hold'em, a blindsteal is a raise with a weak hand from a late position when all other players have folded. An attack on the blinds, or an attack by the small blind on the big blind, is done with the idea that the blinds will fold, leaving the thief with the blinds without having to fight for them.
body.paragraph2: The strategic counterpart of the blindsteal is the blind defense, where the blinds are defended with a weak hand against such a blind steal attempt.

Blockers are cards that a player needs to complete his draw but that are already in an opponent's hand.
Player A: 6 Spades, J Clubs
body.paragraph2: Player B: K Spades, 7 Hearts
body.paragraph3: Flop: 8 Hearts, 5 Diamonds, 4 Clubs
body.paragraph4: Both players have inside straight draws. Player A needs a seven and B a six to make the straight 45678. Hence, they each hold a card that the other needs; A has B's six and B has A's seven. These cards are blockers. There are no longer 4 sixes and 4 sevens available for completing the draws, but just 3. This reduces the chances that either player successfully completes the straight.

A type of preflop raise. A bet placed out of position with the intention of preventing the opponent from making a larger bet.

A bluff is a move in which a player tries to convince the opposition that his hand is stronger (or weaker) than it actually is. It can consist of many actions. The bluff is an attempt to get the opposition to believe something untrue to get him to act on it; either by folding or by betting large amounts against a strong hand, because he doesn't believe it to be strong. A bluff can therefore take on many forms and isn't always an attempt to get an opponent to fold.

We distinguish between pure bluffs, where a player can only win by getting the opponent to fold, and semi bluffs, where a player has a poor hand initially, but one that might develop into a winning hand later: like a flush draw.
body.paragraph2: The profitability of a semi bluff comes from the combined chances that either the opponent will fold to your bet, so your hand will develop into strong one, or the opponent will bet more chips because he does not believe your bet.
body.paragraph3: Slow play, or deceptive play pretending that a good hand is weak, is also considered a bluff.

body.paragraph1: Bluff Call, Dark Tunnel Bluff, Post Oak Bluff, Slowplay

A bluff constitues a series of moves to pretend to have a hand that you do not hold. The bluff call is part of this series and usually consists of winning the pot with a weak hand by bringing the opposition to fold in a later round. A bluff call might be made to give the impression that you are slow playing or might be meant to represent a draw.
body.paragraph2: One use of the bluff call is the float. This is the calling of a continuation bet after a raise, in order to snatch the pot in a future round, should the opposition check or show other signs of a readiness to fold.

body.paragraph1: Bluff, Float

A bluff catcher is a hand that can only beat a bluff. It also means a very passive and transparent player. This players only wins if his opponents bluff too much. Most bluff catchers don't play their own good hands aggressively, but win when their opponents bet on something or bluff often or at the wrong time.
Bluff
This is the number of hands that don't have any real value left on the showdown, but might still be good enough to catch the bluff of an opponent, i.e. you beat his bluff.

You put your opponent on a flush draw on the flop, but it hasn't arrived by the river. You don't have a made hand and assume that your opponent will bluff the river with his busted flush draw every now and then. You need to ask yourself how often and with what range he will do so and accordingly choose the range with which you can catch a bluff.
To induce a bluff is to make a move with a good hand that is intended to draw a bluff from an opponent. A player signals weakness or readiness to fold in order to provoke a bluff.
Bluff
In games with communal cards, the board is all of the community cards, collectively. For example, in Texas Hold'em the board consists of the flop, turn, and river.
Community Cards
The structure of the community cards. Used to describe the potential of Community cards in developing hand styles such as draws.

body.paragraph1: A bonus whore is a player who goes from one online platform to the next just for the deposit bonuses. He makes a deposit with a platform and then plays until he gets the bonus, then he moves on.

Bottom pair is the term used in formats that use community cards, such as Texas Hold'em or Omaha. The bottom pair is made up of the lowest board card and one of the player's cards. More generally speaking, a bottom pair can also refer to a pair which uses one of the smaller community cards to make a pair.

The highest place in a tournament that gets no prize money is called the bubble. If the first ten places in a tournament get a prize, then the 11th place is the bubble. The player who gets this place is called the bubble boy.
body.paragraph2: The other meaning of bubble is the phase of a tournament just before the bubble is reached, where only a few players need to be eliminated before reaching the rewarded places.

The bubble factor is the factor by which your odds in chips differ from your odds in dollars.

Before a new card is dealt, one card must be burned, or put to one side. This card is the burn card. It prevents the knowledge of what this card might be from affecting the game. In Texas Hold'em and Omaha, a card is burned before the starting hands are dealt, and before the flop, turn, and river. In Seven Card Stud, a card is burned before the hands are dealt and in between each street.
Dealer, Deck
To bust or to bust out is to lose your entire stack, or to be eliminated from a tournament. To bust somebody is to eliminate them from a table or tournament.
Broke, Turnier
The button or dealer button is a chip that shows who the current dealer is. The position where the dealer sits is also called the button. We also say that the dealer is on the button.
Dealer, Cutoff, Under the Gun, Early Position, Middle Position, Late Position
dealer's button
The buy-in is the amount that a player must pay to enter a game. In a cash game, it is the amount he must bring to the table.
body.paragraph2: In a tournament, it is the amount he must pay into the prize pool. The whole amount paid to enter the tournament is the buy-in plus the entry fee, where the entry fee goes directly to the host of the tournament. A tournament advertised as "$10+1" means that a player pays 11 dollars in all to enter the tournament, 10 for the prize pool and 1 to the host.

A call is one action a player can make when faced with an opposing bet. It means that he wagers exactly the amount of the opposing bet.

A calling station, or station for short, is an extremely loose and passive player who plays too many hands and goes too far with them. He calls every bet, even with marginal hands and is not able to see when he's beaten. He also plays good hands passively, meaning: he rarely makes a bet to build up a pot or protect a vulnerable hand. They are named calling stations because the call is their favorite move. Hence, they are not bluffable.

There may be many reasons why calling stations play this way. For one, they cannot tell the the strength of their hands and so they go too far with weak hands and do not play their strong hands aggressively enough. On the other hand, they could be looking more for the social aspect of poker; they just want to be involved in the play instead of creating an aggressive atmosphere. A player might also be a calling station because he lacks the confidence to do otherwise, seeing a bluff behind every opposing bet.
Maniac, TAG, LAG, Passive, Aggressive
Station
The cap is the last possible raise in a round or the upper limit on how much can be wagered in one hand. Rounds are usually capped at 3,4, or 5 raises per round. The last raise is the cap.
body.paragraph2: There is a particular form of pot- and no-limit game where a cap limits how much one particular player can wager over all the betting rounds. If he reaches this limit, he is considered all-in.

body.paragraph1: Raise
A player is card dead if he doesn't get many or any good cards for a long time.
Cold Deck
The card removal effect refers to the fact that your own hand often makes it less likely that your opponent has certain hands or certain hand combinations.
body.paragraph2: If you have AK for example, it is somewhat less likely that your opponent has A- and K-hands, since your own hand blocks two of the eight existing relevant cards (4x A, 4x K). This consequently has implications for the range of your opponent.

body.paragraph1: Cash games and tournaments are different poker variations. The participants play for and make wagers in cash. The participants buy into a table and can bring in new money to the table at any time, so long as they do not step over the maximum buy-in for the table.
body.paragraph2: No-limit and Pot-limit games are often called cash games since the players bet as much as they want, as opposed to fixed limit variants where the bet sizes are constrained to discrete steps. Fixed-limit is not viewed as "real" poker in this sense.

To cash out is to get paid. The term is used when withdrawing money from a poker account, for trading in casino chips for money, or when a player leaves a table and the money he had at the table is credited back to his account.
Buy-In, Bankroll
withdrawal

To chase is to call an opposing bet with a hand like a draw; one that has little chance of developing into a winning hand or that carries a negative expectation based on the amount that must be paid. To chase is to fish for a lucky card, and this is not profitable in the long term.
Equity Draw

Cheating is any kind of treacherous or unfair manipulation of the game. In poker, it might be done by fixing the cards, making agreements with other players, hiding cards, stealing chips, etc. In the context of online games, collusion and pokerbots (programs that play poker), as well as global databases of player data are the main forms of cheating. Different platforms have different views on data mining, which is the automated observation of players for the purpose of gathering statistics on them, to be later used in programs like Pokertracker or Pokeroffice.
body.paragraph2: Online poker platforms go to great pains to curb cheating by looking for suspicous patterns in a client's played hands. They also check for banned processes running on the client's machine.

body.paragraph1: Collusion, Bot

A check is one possible action that a player can make when he is not confronted by any opposing bet. To check means to pass the action to the next player without making a wager. A player can only check if no bets have been made.
Call, Bet, Raise, Fold, Check behind, Check in the Dark
Check behind is when the last player to act checks after the player(s) before him have already checked. This means that the action goes to the next street or to the showdown without further wagers.
Player A checks
body.paragraph2: Player B checks (behind)

body.paragraph1: Check down means that players participating in a hand just check all the way to showdown without placing any bets. This happens in very passive games where no one has any cards and no confidence to place a bet.

A check in the dark is like a bet in the dark, but with a check. It means to announce that you are going to check prior to a betting round. A player declares that he will check in the next round, regardless of which cards are dealt. If, for example, a player in Hold'em announces during pre-flop that he will check the flop, then he will check the next round, regardless of what the flop brings.
Check, Bet in the Dark
A check raise is a combination of moves consisting of a player first checking and then raising if a bet is made behind him.
Player A checks

body.paragraph4: A check raise can have many outcomes. It could be intentioned to trap the opponent. It also demonstrates strength and can be used as a bluff.
body.paragraph5: In some casinos and home games, check raises are considered impolite and are not allowed.

A coinflip refers to a situation in which two players have almost equal chances of winning. Since it's close to a 50:50 probability, the game may as well be decided by flipping a coin.
Player A: A Spades, K Clubs
body.paragraph2: Player B: Q Spades, Q Diamonds
body.paragraph3: A classic coinflip in Hold'em happens when a pair before the pre-flop is pitted against two overcards. The chances are around 44:56, with a slight advantage for the queens.

A cold call is calling a raise after there was a bet and one or more raises. A player cold calls when he calls more than one bet at once. A cold call occurs in Texas Hold'em when a player raises in a pre-flop round and another player calls it. There was just one raise, but the blinds also count as a bet.
Player A bets.
body.paragraph2: Player B raises

Collusion is an agreement between two or more players to swap information about their cards, either via telephone, chat programs, or in a casino with secret signals.
body.paragraph2: Online platforms have efficient protection mechanisms against collusion, among others checking for particular patterns in hands played.

body.paragraph1: Cheating
A player is pot committed or committed if he has already invested so many chips in the pot that he can no longer fold if he is raised all-in.
body.paragraph2: Contrary to the common misconception, this is not based on the fact that a player has already invested a large part of his stack but rather that he has very good odds for a call.
body.paragraph3: For example, if a player has invested half of his stack in the pot and another player puts him all-in, then he will get pot odds of at least 3:1, meaning he must only win 25% of the time to break even. If there is other dead money in the pot, then his odds are even better.
body.paragraph4: If the player believes himself to be so far behind that he no longer has correct odds for a call, it is still correct to fold even if he has already invested a lot. It is wrong to call a raise just because you have already invested a lot of money in the pot, since money put into the pot no longer belongs to anybody.

body.paragraph1: Pot Odds

Community or board cards are those cards dealt face up on the table, which every player may see and use. They belong to all the players, hence they are communal. In Texas Hold'em, for example, the three flop cards, turn card, and river card are the community cards.
Board, Exposed Card
Board
To complete is to make a bet in order to match the forced bet balance on a table, or to match all-in raise balance (where the raiser does not have the required chips for a minimum raise).
Player A raises by 40 chips.
body.paragraph2: Player B would have to wager 80 chips for a normal minimum raise: the 40 to match player A plus an additional 40, but he only has 60 and goes all-in with these. A can only complete by matching B's 20 chips. If a third player were in the hand, A could, depending on the house rules, decide not to raise again becasue B's raise was irregular; A can only complete it.
body.paragraph3: In games with blinds, a complete is when the small blind pays the difference between the small blind and the big blind to stay in the hand, provided there have been no raises by the time it is his turn. The big blind is a complete bet, i.e. the amount that a player must wager to stay in the hand. Since the small blind only pays half the big blind, his bet is incomplete.
body.paragraph4: In games with an ante structure, a player can choose to bet the full amount instead of the smaller bring-in.

body.paragraph1: All-In, Ante, Blind, Bet

Connectivity or connectedness is a property that a group of cards can have describing how sequential the cards in the group are. For example, if the board cards in Texas Hold'em are close to one another in value, then they are said to be connected. High connectivity means the cards are sequential, like king, queen, jack. A low connectivity means the cards are not close to one another.
body.paragraph2: Straight draws are only possible with a high connectivity, and higher connectivity means more straights are possible. Hence, players like to play connectors like 78 in Texas Hold'em and Omaha since they make it easier to hit a straight.

body.paragraph1: Suitedness, High Card Value, Connectors

Connectors are sequential cards like 87, 54, 32. If they are of the same suit, then they are called suited connectors, otherwise they are unsuited connectors. There are also one-gap connectors like 53 or 86, and two-gap connectors like 52 and 96.
body.paragraph2: Connectors are strong because it is easy to make straight draws with them. The closer the cards are together, the more possibilities for straight draws exist. This is easy to see if you just list out all the straights with 87 compared to those with 96.
body.paragraph3: There are 4 with 87:

body.paragraph1: 3 Spades, 4 Hearts
body.paragraph2: 5 Hearts, 6 Spades
body.paragraph3: 8 Spades, 9 Hearts

body.paragraph1: Suited Connectors, Straight Draw

If a player bets in an earlier round and then bets again in the current one, this is called a continuation bet since it continues the aggression from the previous round. A typical continuation bet situation is when a player raises in the first round and is called by another player and then bets again after the flop, often as a bluff when having nothing at all.

A delayed continuation bet is one that is not made directly in the next round but rather two rounds from the original bet. For instance, if a player raises pre-flop in Texas Hold'em, does not bet after the flop but bets after the turn, it is known as a delayed continuation bet.
Raise Bluff

A cooler is a hand lost not because of a mistake but rather because of the way the cards came out; a player with a strong hand loses to another player with a stronger hand. The losing player usually loses a lot of money and there is no way to avoid it with correct play.
body.paragraph2: An example of a cooler is when two players in Texas Hold'em both have a high pair and each hit their three of a kind on the flop. It's not a bad beat, just bad luck that the cards were such that correct play on the part of both players leads to one losing terribly. Both players had very strong hands.

body.paragraph1: Bad Beat, Cold Deck
To cover somebody is to have more chips than him. A player with more chips can call a covered player's all-in and still have chips left if he loses.
body.paragraph2: This is important in tournament play, where any covering player could be the one that eliminates the covered player.

body.paragraph1: Stack, All-In
A starting hand of two kings in Texas Hold'em is called cowboys.
Hole Cards
Kings

A player or his stack is called crippled if he loses most of his chips. The term is used in tournaments when a player loses most of his stack and can't do much, since he doesn't have the chips to make complex moves.
Stack, Turnier
A crying call is a call made in the final round in the face of overwhelming probability of defeat. Nevertheless, the call is made because of good pot odds and folding is not an option.
Call
Desperation call
The cut refers to the profit distribution in the event of a win after the makeup has been paid back to the stakers.
body.paragraph2: The cut can be used in full stakings as well as partial stakings.

body.paragraph1: Player A wants a full staking for a tournament package with a cut of 50:50. This means that after paying back the makeup, the winnings are distributed in equal parts.

Player A wants a cash game staking, invests $1,000 himself and would like another $1,000 provided by others. The cut now shifts from the standardised 50/50 or 60/40 towards the stakee, since he will have to carry the costs caused by a potential loss.
In games with a dealer button, the cutoff is the position immediately to the right of the button.
Data mining refers to the use of software that gathers information on hands played by third party players, for games in which you don't take part.
body.paragraph2: These programs observe various tables and take in thousands of hands, so that a player can later analise them and incorporate this knowledge into his game. In this way, it is possible to know something about an opponent's style of play without ever having encountered him on the tables.
body.paragraph3: Data mining is forbidden by many online platforms, as it is viewed as cheating. It is therefore subject to the same kind of regulations as pokerbots, e.g. inspection of running processes on a client's machine for suspicous activity. In general, the use of central databases pulled from the internet or available for a monthly fee, is also forbidden.

body.paragraph1: Cheating

If a player misses paying his blinds and then returns to the game, he must, depending on house rules, pay both the big blind for returning to the game and also the small blind. The latter is called a dead blind, since it is not counted as a wager by the player but is more of a penalty that goes directly into the pot.
Blind Dead Money
Dead cards are cards that are no longer in the game because they were already folded.
Dead money in general is money in the pot that never belonged to any active player, or money that belongs to an active player who will probably fold.
body.paragraph2: Dead money is therefore a combination of dead blinds and bets by players who have folded or will fold. This kind of dead money improves the pot odds directly, since it means a player can win more for his bet than just the wagers of his active opponents.
body.paragraph3: Money wagered by players who barely have a chance of winning or who, it can be presumed will lose all their money if they stay at the table, is also called dead. In this sense, the term can also be used as slang for bad player.

body.paragraph1: Dead, Blind, Pot Odds, Fish

A deal or final table deal is an early end to a large tournament that comes about when all the remaining players agree to definitionide the prize money in some way. The distribution of prize money is usually based on the chip counts. It is also common to dole out most of the prize money and to then play the rest of the tournament to see who wins.
Tournament, MTT, Final Table, ICM
The dealer is the one who gives out the cards.
body.paragraph2: He doesn't actually have to be dealing the cards, but he is the one who would do it according to the rules, and the order of action therefore progresses as if he were dealing.
body.paragraph3: In Texas Hold'em, each player therefore takes a turn being dealer, even if the one actually giving out the cards is a casino employee or software program. The dealer designation moves clockwise around the table from round to round, even though the actual dealing is not performed by the players. At every stage the current dealer is denoted by a small chip, called the dealer button, which also moves around the table in clockwise fashion.

body.paragraph1: Deception is a broad term for all sorts of tricks used to fool an opponent into believing something untrue, or into making an incorrect move based on a false pretence. A move made for deception has the intention of confusing your opponents regarding the strength or type of your hand.
body.paragraph2: Deceptive play describes this kind of move. Deceptive play can involve bluffing, semi-bluffing, slow play and advertising moves.

body.paragraph1: Bluff, Advertise, Slowplay
The deck refers to the stack of cards.

A stack is deep if it is substantially larger than the maximum table buy-in. If a player has 200 dollars at a table where the maximum buy-in is 100 dollars, his stack is deep. Such a player can win money from other deep stacks, but is also vulnerable to losing a lot of money at once.
body.paragraph2:
This situation has an impact on the style of play and strategy, since a player with a deep stack can get better implied odds and can undertake cost-intensive moves. The cost-benefit ratio, based on the idea of pot odds and implied odds, is shifted because the costs may be higher in relation to the possible winnings.
In a tournament, a stack is deep if it is large in comparison to the other stacks at the table and to the forced bets.

body.paragraph1: Shortstack, Turnier, Implied Odds
A deposit is a payment made into an account, in this case to an online account with a poker platform or online casino. The account is also called a bankroll.
Cash out, Bankroll
One of the four suits used in Poker cards.
Clubs, Hearts, Spades

Disconnect protection is a measure whereby a player's bets in an online game are protected if his internet connection fails. The usual method is to treat such a player as all-in; he makes no more actions and participates in the showdown.
body.paragraph2: This function varies from platform to platform. Usually, there is a limit to the number of times this will work for a given player. Often it is not available in tournaments; the disconnected player's cards are folded and he loses the bets he's made so far.
body.paragraph3: These limitations are to prevent abuse of the feature, since a player could just disconnect his machine if the wagers become too high but he does not want to fold. Such disconnect protection abuse is seen as cheating by some platforms, but it is very difficult to prove.

Discounted outs are outs with those cards subtracted that would improve the hand but not improve it to the best hand, or that might improve an opposing hand more.
Player: 2 Clubs, 3 Clubs
body.paragraph2: Flop: 3 Hearts, 5 Hearts, K Diamonds
body.paragraph3: The player has a draw to a straight. Any ace or six on the turn or river will complete his hand. Those are his outs and he can start out presuming to have 8 outs; four aces and four sixes.
body.paragraph4: But what if an opponent had this:
body.paragraph5: Q Hearts, J Hearts
body.paragraph6: In this case, the ace or six of hearts would give his opponent a flush. These two outs are not clean, and so they must be discounted.
body.paragraph7: An opponent might also have this hand:
body.paragraph9: In this case, any six would give his opponent a better straight, meaning that only the four aces are clean and so he must discount 4 of his original 8 outs.

body.paragraph1: Whereas it is difficult to say, in game, what an opponent might have, there is always a certain probability that he has one of the better possible draws. The more players are in the game, the more likely it is that one of them has such a hand. The more playable are the cards that would complete your draw, the more likely are players to hold them. Hence, the strictness with which one must discount one's outs are contingent on these factors. The discounted outs take into account the possibility that even though the draw may complete, it may not win the hand. They keep the calculation of one's chances to win with odds and outs simple.
Outs, Odds
modified outs

In games with community cards, a hand is dominated by an opposing hand if it is not a pair and the highest cards are the same but the opposing hand has a higher kicker. The effect is that for any pair made with the highest card, the opposing player will always win because of his superior kicker.

A Double or Nothing tournament is a special variant of a Sit and Go. One half of the players win double the buy-in while the other half is eliminated from the tournament.
Sit and Go
DoN

The term donkbet comes from the combination of donkey and bet. Such a bet is an unexpected or risky bet by a player who was not the aggressor in the previous round, often into a player who was the aggressor. This is also called a gaybet or bet out of nowhere.
body.paragraph2: A typical situation for a donkbet is when a player calls a raise in the first round then bets into the raiser in the next round.
body.paragraph3: Donkbets can be dumb moves by inexperienced players, or deceptive maneouvers made to trick an opponent since they are often viewed as weak. Furthermore, they are used as blockbets in variants with variable bet sizes, where a small bet can be made to keep an opponent from making a large, pot odds-spoiling bet.

Gaybet
Reckless novice players are called "donk".
In Seven Card Stud, every player gets two downcards and two open cards as a starting hand. The open card is called the door card.
Seven Card Stud

When a player doubles his stack in single hand, this is called a double-up. It can happen, for instance, when a player goes all-in and wins after being called by an opponent with at least as large a stack as him.
All-In
A downcard is a card dealt face down on the table that is only visible to one player. The starting cards in Texas hold'em or the final, seventh card in Seven Card Stud are downcards.
Exposed Card
A downswing is a phase or period of time in which a player sustains extended losses due to bad luck.

Downswings are natural occurrences that belong to a game of chance, just like their counterpart the upswing, where one makes extended gains. Any game with an element of chance has a certain amount of variance since the behavior of the cards is randomized for short periods of time and will deviate from the normal distribution. In the long term, however, the behavior will settle down even if a downswing can last for more than ten thousand hands. Secondary factors can also enter play, like tilt or the influence of frustration on play.
body.paragraph2: The ability and mental fortitude needed to deal with long streaks of bad luck are the signs of a good poker player. A solid bankroll management scheme is necessary so that a player still has enough money at the end of the streak to begin making profit again.
body.paragraph3: The most common mistake is that players go on tilt during downswing and increase their losses unneccessarily, exhausting their bankroll so that they can no longer recover when the bad luck lets up.

body.paragraph1: Tilt, Swing, Upswing, Variance
To draw is to play a draw, an unfinished hand that needs one or more cards to turn into a made hand.
Draw
A player is drawing dead if there are no cards that will bestow upon him the winning hand, should they be dealt.
Draw, Drawing live, Drawing thin
Drawing live, as opposed to drawing dead, means that the draw still has a good chance to win the hand. There are still cards in the deck, which, if dealt, would make the draw the best hand.
Draw, Drawing dead, Drawing thin

In contrast to drawing dead, a player who is drawing thin still has a slim chance to make the best hand by the river. The mathematical probability of victory is small, however, since there are very few cards that would bring victory.
Draw, Drawing dead, Drawing live

A board on the flop or on the turn is referred to as drawy or draw heavy if it makes a lot of draws possible, i.e. if there are many cards that could complete the draws of the involved players on the upcoming street.
9 Spades, 8 Spades, 4 Diamonds
body.paragraph2: On this board many players could have flush draw, straight draw or gutshot combinations for example. There are many so-called outs that could complete the common draws on this flop on the turn or on the river. The board is therefore draw heavy.
body.paragraph3: K Diamonds, 8 Hearts, 2 Clubs
body.paragraph4: This on the other hand is a very dry board and only offers the player few options to considerably improve his hand on the turn and on the river. It is not drawy.

A dry board describes a board that makes no or only few draws possible. In contrast to wet or draw-heavy boards, it is not at all or only barely connected and rainbow, i.e. it has three different suits. The fewer draws are possible on a board, the drier it is.
Dry Board

In games where the relative position of a player to the dealer determines the order of play, the early positions are those who must act first. At a full ring table in Texas Hold'em, for instance, the first three positions after the blinds are the early positions, also called UTG (under the gun), UTG+1, and UTG+2.
body.paragraph2: The disadvantage of players in early position is that they must act early in the round and so they don't know what the other players will do. The more players that will act after a certain player, the heavier the disadvantage of information weighs upon him, and the more probable it is that a player behind him has a strong hand. It is more difficult to craft a game against opponents acting later than against those who have already acted.

body.paragraph1: In Position, Out of Position, Middle Position, Late Position

Having an edge means having an advantage over another player. For example, a superior hand has an edge over weaker hands. A player with superior skills or position at the table also has an edge.
The edge odds are a measure for the average edge of a player in one or more games:
body.paragraph2: Edge Odds = average winnings / average winnings of the biggest winner
body.paragraph3: A good player has edge odds in the range of .25 to .65.

If one player has 10,000 chips and another only 5,500 chips, the most either player can win from the other in the hand is 5,500.

The entry fee is the money paid to participate in a tournament that goes to the host of the tournament and is not put into the prize pool. The cost for participating in the tournament is therefore buy-in + entry fee, where only the buy-in goes into the prize pool. Hence, a tournament on an online platform billed as "$10+$1" would mean that a player must pay $11 to participate in the tournament but only $10 goes into the prize pool. The other $1 goes to the platform.
Turnier, Buy-In
The equity or pot equity refers to the share of the pot belonging to a player based on his long term chances to win.
Expected Value, Pot Odds, Odds
The expected value or expectation, abbreviated EV, of a move is the average gain or loss that results from a situation taking into account all possible outcomes and their probabilities.

To calculate the expected value, one must know the probabilities and payoffs of all possible outcomes give what has already transpired. The EV is the sum over all possible outcomes of the product of the payoff of the outcome and the probability of that outcome.
body.paragraph2: EV = P(outcome 1) * Payoff 1 + P(Outcome 2) * Payoff 2 + ... + P(Outcome n) * Payoff n

Exploiting refers to the process of identifying the leaks of your opponents. Knowing how your opponents play can considerably influence your decision on which line you want to play. Especially in marginal situations, you can and should use so-called reads in the decision making process. For example, it can be useful to know in which spots the opponent plays weak, when he is aggressive and when he is vulnerable to bluffs – or which reads he himself has on the other players. In order to collect such information, the player does not necessarily have to be dealt into the hand - he should also pay attention to the action when he is not involved.
Exposed cards are any which are dealt face up for all to see, e.g. the board cards in Texas Hold'em or the door card of a player in Seven Card Stud.
Downcard

The term face cards refers to cards which depict persons.  In a deck of modern playing there are therefore 3 different types of face cards: kings, queens and jacks. They are also known as 'paint cards'.
In a tournament that begins on multiple tables, the last remaining table where the tournament is decided is called the final table.
MTT

A player acts first in if he is the first player to enter the betting round by betting or calling. A first in raise or open raise means when a player raises in the first betting round after all preceeding players have folded.
Open Raise, Preflop, First in Vigorish
A player is the first to act if it is his turn to make the first move in a betting round. The player who takes his turn last is last to act.
First in

A fish can be a curse or can mean simply a bad player, against whom play is profitable. Fish can be identified by their lack of strategy and by the moves they make with negative expected value. They play marginal hands and bet too much. It's also an insult, used interchangeably with those classics, retard, idiot, and moron.
body.paragraph2: The opposite of a fish is a shark, a player who robs fish of their money. Barracudas are those players in between, who still make money off the fish, but are good to mediocre players. Very aggressive players are called piranha. A table with a lot of bad players is called an aquarium.

In a fixed limit game you can only bet a fixed amount. Only small bets can be made in the early betting rounds, while large bets are allowed in later betting rounds.

In a $1/$2 Texas Hold'em game, a small bet is a $1 bet, a large bet is a $1 bet, and a large bet is a $2 bet. Only small bets can be made before and on the flop. Players can bet $2 during the turn and river betting rounds, and no more bets are allowed.

A flat or smooth call is calling a bet with a hand that is strong enough for a raise. A flat call is therefore a kind of slowplay.
Raise, Call

A float is a move where an aggressor is called so that a player may retake the initiative in a later round, provided that the original aggressor allows it. It is a broad term. The call on the flop can be a bluff call, smooth call, or a call for pot control. The term is often used in sense of a bluff.

A forced bet is a mandatory wager in the first betting round that exists in various poker variants in different forms, like ante, blind, or bring-in.
body.paragraph2: Antes are forced bets that every player must pay at the beginning of the round before the cards are dealt.
body.paragraph3: Blinds are forced bets that only two players per round must pay before the cards are dealt.
body.paragraph4: The bring-in is a forced bet in stud games that one player must pay after the cards are dealt. Which player pays is decided by the value of the door cards, the cards that a player has face up before him. It could be the player with the highest or the lowest door card.

rabbit hunt

A free card is a card that a player gets to see without paying any more. For example, if there are multiple players in a game of Hold'em and nobody bets on the flop, then all the palyers get to see the turn card without investing any more. It is a freecard.
You get a free play in the big blind if no player raises and you see the flop without having to invest any more money.

Freeroll refers to tournaments that do not have buy-in fees. Freeroll also refers to a situation where two players have equally strong hands, but one player also has a draw that would net him the entire pot if he hits the draw; the second player does not have such a draw.

A full house is a poker hand consisting of a pair and a three of a kind. It is written as full house (three of a kind) over (pair). A full house is also called a boat, full boat, full, or full barn. If two players have a full house, then the one with the the highest three of a kind wins. If they have the same three, then the with the highest pair wins.

A full ring game refers to a round of poker with 7 to 10 players. It is also caleld 10max. In online poker there are also tables with 9 max. Shorthanded tables are tables with 6 or fewer players, also called 6max. Play at a fulll ring table is calmer and less frenzied than at shorthanded tables, for which reason action-oriented players prefer the shorthanded tables so that they can play more hands. It also gives rise to a higher variance.

In a full staking, the stakee sells 100% of his staking offer. The involved parties agree on a cut at the beginning of the staking. Usually, a full staking is played with stakeback.

The term gambling has a few meanings. To gamble can mean to play a game of chance, such as poker. In poker itself, a gambler is a player who does not go about the game in a strategic way but instead relies on luck, like in any other casino game. A gamble can, in general, denote any action that is predicated on great risk.

The gap concept was formulated by David Sklansky. He says that it takes a stronger hand to call a raise than to make it in the first place. There is a gap between the hands with which one could raise and the ones with which one could call a raise.
body.paragraph2: This is a description of the fact that one could go all-in with many more hands than one could call an opposing all-in in the late phases of a tournament. Likewise, there is a larger range of hands with which one can steal the blinds or antes than with which one can defend them.
body.paragraph3: A consequence of the gap concept is that aggressors always have an advantage.

body.paragraph1: Blindsteal
Maximize your profits by betting, raising or re-raising when you are ahead.

To grind is to play consistently for profit on a given limit without taking much risk. A grinder or rounder is a player who plays on a limit beneath his abilities to make part or all of his income, even though he could play on a higher limit at greater risk.
body.paragraph2: The other meaning of grinding is to work up one's bankroll on a lower limit after encountering financial difficulties on a higher one. We say, a player grinds through the limits.

The image of a player is what others think of his style. They could believe him to be passive or aggressive, to be a bluffer, or one that only bets if he has a good hand, and so on. The image has a direct impact on the actions taken by the other players, and on how best to provoke a certain reaction from them.
body.paragraph2: The image becomes increasingly important as the level of play increases. The aim of building up an image is to lead the opposition into incorrect beliefs and moves. Hence, a player with a solid, conservative image can bluff more often since his opponents will believe him to have a good hand, whereas a player who is believed to be wild will have an easier time getting his good hands to pay off.

Implied odds or implied pot odds are modified pot odds used when playing a draw. They account for the fact that more money can be extracted from the opposition if the draw hits. They include the bets that the opposition will make, not just what is already in the pot.
body.paragraph2: Implied Odds = expected possible winnings : bets to be paid
body.paragraph3: Implied odds therefore include a speculative element: How much money can I extract from my opponent if I complete my draw?
body.paragraph4: Moves in No-limit games are often reckoned with implied odds, since in the best case you could win your opponent's whole stack. A typical application is calling on set value in Texas Hold'em, where a player calls an opposing raise with a pair in his hole cards, knowing that he will hit his set around 12% of the time on the flop. Since he would then have a strong hand and a good chance at winning a large pot, it makes sense for him to call. If he would only consider the money currently in the pot, he would often not have proper odds for a call.

The independent chip model or ICM is a mathematical model for play in the late phase of a tournament with relatively small stacks. It is based on the idea that every chip represents a chance to land in the money and thereby to make a profit. Each additional chip improves a player's position in the tournament and betters his resulting profit. The chips in a stack of 10000 are simply worth more than those in a stack of 100, which will be all-in by default if a blind of that size must be paid.

In a game for real money, every action has an EV that says what kind of gain or loss can be expected to result from the action.  In a tournament, this translates to how many chips one can expect to gain or lose.
body.paragraph2: But in a tournament the chips are just a means to an end, i.e. they are just capital used to win money. For this reason, the EVof an action must be modified to consider how much actual money will bemade as a result of the action, and this is called $EV.
body.paragraph3: An action with positive EV could have a negative $EV, especially in cases where an action would put a player all-in. Losing an all-in means elimination from the tournament and a loss of the buy-in. Hence, the ICM is useful in the push-or-fold phase of a tournament, where a player must decide whether to push or call all-in or to fold.

body.paragraph1: The $EV depends on many factors:
body.paragraph2: 1. The size of your own stack
body.paragraph3: 2. The type and strength of your opponents
body.paragraph4: 3. Your own gaming skills
body.paragraph5: 4. Your position
body.paragraph6: 5. THe distribution of stacks at the table
body.paragraph8: Using these as starting points, we can make some assumptions about theprobability with which one will obtain an arbitrary placement in the tournament. These probabilities and the payoff structure allow us to compute the $EV.

body.paragraph1: In a given situation, then, one must calculate for each possible actionthe $EV and compare the results. If the possible actions are all-in or fold, then one must calculate the $EV for the cases where:
body.paragraph2: 1. you fold
body.paragraph3: 2. you go all-in, are called and lose
body.paragraph4: 3. you go all-in, are called and win
body.paragraph5: 4. you go all-in and are not called
body.paragraph6: The probability that you will win or lose against a particular opponent must be calculated by estimating the all-in calling range of that opponent. The sum of every possible outcome of an action weighted by its probability will tell you what the $EV of the action is, and this will tell you whether to fold or stay.
body.paragraph7: These calculations are too complex to make during play. They are meant toprovide a theoretical basis for play, and in particular to comment onwith which hand ranges one can go or call all-in. There are also programs that will perform ICM calculations for you.

body.paragraph2: In poker tournaments the momentum is often among the decisive factors between victory and defeat. A player who managed to double his stack multiple times in a short period of time has positive momentumon his side. These successes make him feel stronger, enabling him toplay confidently, dominate the table and keep building his stack.
body.paragraph3: The momentum can also have negative effects on a player. A poker player experiencing a downswing will know this phenomenon: He can't seem to remember the last time that the boardhelped him. This can lead to wrong decisions due to a lack ofconcentration and incorrect adjustments to his game. It is difficult forhim to put an end to the negative series of events since hisself-confidence is continuously decreasing with the win rate. The playerexpects the next coin flip to go wrong, the next monster draw not to arrive and the next session to be another unsuccessful one. A negative momentum can have a long-term impact on the decisions of a player.
body.paragraph4: Players should not let past decisions have a negative effect on their mindset. A player who is experiencing negative momentumhas to accept the current situation, look ahead and make new decisionswithout giving any thought to irreversible losses from the past.

To overplay a hand is to invest more money in it than it is worth. This often happens when a player has an initially strong hand and takes the initiative, but fails to recognize the point where his hand has lost its strength. At this point, he should reduce his aggression and attempt to control the pot.
Pot Control
A player has a set if he makes a three of a kind with two of his cards and one of the board's. A player has an overset if he has a set that is higher than another player's set.

Partial staking means that a stakee sells pieces of between 1% and 99% of his staking offer at a predetermined rate. Some players might charge a markup. The involved parties also agree on a percentage that specifies the cut.
body.paragraph2: Another type of partial staking is a situation for example, where the stakee invests money himself (for example in a cash game staking). In this case, both parties provide a part of the bankroll and the stakee carries a residual risk. Again, the involved parties agree on a cut at the beginning of the staking.

body.paragraph1: A player is passive if he seldom raises or bets. He plays his hands less aggressively and lets other players take the initiative. He reacts more than he acts.
Aggressive
To call an opponent's bet with losing cards.
The payout is a reward set by a tournament for a specific ranking.
Payout Structure, Tournaments
The payout structure of a tournament determines how the prize money is definitionided between the winners, and how many players will actually win money.
body.paragraph2: A typical payout structure for a sit'n'go:
body.paragraph3: 1st place, 50% of pool
body.paragraph4: 2nd place, 30% of pool
body.paragraph5: 3rd place, 20% of pool

The term perceived rangerefers to the number of hands that your  opponent thinks you could havein a certain playing situation.  Irrelevant of your actual range, your opponent can have a very different  impression of your style of play and his estimations about your range  therefore don't always have to be correct.

The playability of a hand is a broad measure of how much a handlends itself to being played. The more complext the arrangement ofcards, the harder it is to evaluate the strength of one's own hand and the blurrier the situation, the less the playability. A hand with a high playabilityis one for which one can say with relative ease where one stands. Veryplayable hands also do not tend to be ones for which it is easy to avoidbeing dominated or coming in second.
Dominated
In Texas Hold'em, a pocket pair is a pair made from both of the player's hole cards. For example, if a player has two kings, then he has pocket kings.
Pair
A starting hand of two aces in Texas Hold'em is called pocket rockets.
Hole Cards.

Poker is a family of cardgames sharing a common goal for the players ofmaking the best combination of cards and placing wagers on those cardsduring the rounds of the game. The bets are paid into a pot and the player with the best combination of cards at the end of the hand wins the pot. Usually a hand consists of five cards.
body.paragraph2: Poker is a game of chance, but there are elements of strategy to it. The strategies depend on whether one plays against a dealer in a casino or against other players, against whom one can gain an advantage through careful manoeuvering.
body.paragraph3: The word is also a synonym for four of a kind.

body.paragraph1: There are countless variants of poker.
body.paragraph2: The most common variants can be classified as follows:

body.paragraph1: Each player recieves some cards of his own face down at the beginning of a hand. In later rounds, further cards are dealt face up on the table and these belong to all the players. Any player may use these community cards to build a hand. The most popular versions of this type are Texas Hold'em and Omaha.

There are no community cards in stud poker. Each player gets new cards over the course of a hand and bets follow dealing of the cards. In Seven Card Stud,for instance, each player gets two downcards and an open card to start.There are four other betting rounds, where the players get three moreopen cards and one downcard. From these seven cards, the player must build a hand of five. His opponents can see 4 of his cards but they cannot use them for their hands.

Draw poker is the opposite extreme from games with community cards. Each player gets only downcards, so that his opponents have no direct knowledge of them. One or more draw rounds follow, where players can trade cards in their hand for new ones.
body.paragraph2: Five Card draw is the most well known of these variants, and even non-poker players know it from westerns.

body.paragraph1: Low games are those in which the worst hand and not the best wins. Examples are Razz, Lowball Draw, and Deuce to Seven Triple Draw.
Hi/Lo games are those where both the best and the worst hands win; they split the pot. Omaha and Seven Cards Stud are both played with Hi/Lo variants.
Pokerhands, Texas Hold'em, Omaha, Seven Card Stud
A face that does not reveal any expression or information.
As the name suggests, a poker school is a real or virtual space where people can learn how to play poker.
Poker Strategy

Poker was often associated with gambling, stacks of cards, dirty places,and smoke-filled rooms in the past, but in the modern development ofpoker, it is recognized as a unique strategic game. Poker is a gamewhere thoughtful consideration and strategic action allow good playersto defeat poor players in the long run. There is no single, unchangingpoker strategy - instead, there are many ways and methods of playingbased on mathematics, probability, and player psychology.
Poker School

A pokerbot is an autonomous piece of software that plays poker. This simulated player is called a pokerbot, or bot. The program can play one or more tables at once and makes decisions based on certain algorithms.

The main uses for such software are as training programs and for online play, where the bot plays against people. Most poker platforms and players consider the latter cheating,and the platform operators take measures to curb their use. There is aconsiderable group of programmers working on such bots, mainly out ofscientific and technical interest. They also work on other games, likebackgammon and chess, pitting their programs against humans. There arealso programmers whose aim it is to make money off the bots.

Since it is difficult to talk about them without specialized knowledge,there is a certain amount of legend and controversy surrounding thestate of these bots. it is sure that there are profitable bots on thelower limits in fixed-limit and no-limitHold'em cash games and tournaments. This is probably because theplayers on these limits are not very good. Programs developed in theacademic sphere, like Vex Bot, are on par with mid-level players.
Cheating, Collusion

A chip is a plastic disc that represents a particular value. It is used as currency in tournaments or cash games so that the players don't have to shove bills or coins all around the table. The chips can be traded in for money later.
Cash out

A range is polarised if it is skewed either towards very strong hands or very weak hands. The expression "nuts or nothing" describes the polarisation of a range: If a player is trying to represent a full house by making an over-bet on the river for example, the player either has that full house or he doesn't have any showdown equity and is trying to take the pot with a bluff. Since he wouldn't make this move with a medium-strength hand, his range is referred to as polarised.
body.paragraph2: By polarising it, the range only contains two opposing types of hands (value hands and air), which makes it prone to exploitation. A player should therefore keep his range as wide as possible, allowing him to represent different hand strengths. This process is called merging your range (merged range).

body.paragraph1: The player's position at the table.
In Texas Hold'em and Omaha, the postflop is the part of the game that comes after the flop.
Preflop

The pot is the prize pool consisting of all the bets made in a hand. If the potis definitionided between two or more players, e.g. in Hi/Lo variants in whichthe best and worst hands win, or because two or more players have thesame hand, this is called a split pot.
body.paragraph2: There can also be more than one pot in the hand, i.e. when one or more players go all-in. All bets made above the all-in amount are put into a new pot, the side pot, so that only the players who have the necessary money can compete for it. The first pot is called the main pot.

body.paragraph1: Main Pot, Side Pot, Split Pot, Protected Pot
A player is pot committed or committed if he has already invested so many chips in the pot that he can no longer fold if he is raised all-in.
body.paragraph2: Contrary to the common misconception, this is not based on the fact that a player has already invested a large part of his stack but rather that he has very good odds for a call.
body.paragraph3: For example, if a player has invested half of his stack in the pot and another player puts him all-in, then he will get pot odds of at least 3:1, meaning he must only win 25% of the time to break even. If there is other dead money in the pot, then his odds are even better.
body.paragraph4: If the player believes himself to be so far behind that he no longer has correct odds for a call, it is still correct to fold even if he has already invested a lot. It is wrong to call a raise just because you have already invested a lot of money in the pot, since money put into the pot no longer belongs to anybody.

body.paragraph1: Pot Odds
A move is described as being for pot control if it's aim is to determine the size of the pot, either by making it large or keeping it small.
body.paragraph2: The latter is usually the case if you have a good but not great hand and do not want to play over a lot of money.
body.paragraph3: Pot control can be effected by manipulating your own bet size, e.g. making it smaller than normal. It can also mean forgoing a bet or calling instead of raising.

body.paragraph1: Line
Pot limit is a betting structure where the size of the bet must be less than the current size of the pot. Typically, the bet amount exceeds the previous bet or raise.
Fixed Limit, No-Limit
The pot oddsare the ratio between possible winnings and the cost to have a chanceat obtaining those winnings. Hence, they are the inverse cost/benefitratio for a given bet.
body.paragraph2: Pot Odds = possible profit : bet amount payable

body.paragraph1: The pot has $10
body.paragraph2: Player A bets $5.
body.paragraph3: There are 15$ in the pot. That is the possible profit. The cost for Player B, who follows A, is $5. Hence, he has pot odds of $15:$5 or 3:1.

body.paragraph1: Pot odds are useful when the question is whether a callis profitable in the long term, e.g. when playing draws. If afterweighting by the probability, the cost is too high or the winnings toolow, then the callis not worth making. In this case, you will not win a large enough sumoften enough to make the investment profitable in the long term.
body.paragraph2: For example, if two players are in the final betting round and the first bets $2 in a pot of $10 while player B can only beat a bluff. Then player A must be bluffing in one of seven cases so that B can call without sustaining long term losses.
body.paragraph3: In this case, the pot odds were 10+2:2 or 6:1. Player B would therefore break even if his odds,or chances of winning were 6:1. He could lose six times (each timepaying $2) and win once (winning $12). Hence, A must be bluffing 14% ofthe time for B to call.

body.paragraph1: Odds, Implied Odds, Equity, Expected Value
In Texas Hold'em and Omaha the part of the game that takes place before the flop is dealt is called the preflop.
Preflop, Flop
A bet made primarily to check your opponents' reactions, in order to gather information. This tends to be a small bet made in pot-limit or no-limit games.
Feeler Bet

A method of play used to stimulate extra action in a poker game. Forinstance, giving a bonus for starting a table in online play (oftendouble points), or in a live game having other players make bets which are not necessarily related to the outcome of the hand.
body.paragraph2: One example of proposition play can be seen on GSN's High Stakes PokerTV show. During the opening episodes of season 4, the players agreed topay a winner of a hand an extra $500 each, if he won with 7-2.

body.paragraph1: A player hired by the casino to stimulate action. Sometimes this playeris bankrolled by the casino, in other cases he earns an hourly wage.
A protected pot is one in which a player is already all-in, meaning that the pot is no longer winable with a bluff, since it is certainly going to a showdown.
Pot, Bluff, Pot committed

The currently strongest made hands often face the problem of being overtaken by an opponent's drawing hand. These hands must be protected against draws. Protection means making the pot odds unattractive for any drawing hands to call, so that they no longer have a profitable play. Protection also means reducing the number of opponents through forcing folds.
Thinning the Field, Pot Odds, Morton's Theorem

A protection bet is one made to protect a vulnerable made hand from a draw by ruining the opposing draw's pot odds, i.e. he must invest too much money to see the next cards so he cannot continue to play his draw.
Protection, Draw, Pot Odds

A pure bluff is a bet made with a weak hand that one would, in all probability, lose. The bet is intended to make the opponent fold. One's own hand has either no or slim chances of development. The primary aim of the bluff is to get the opposition to fold. On a metalevel, however, one can make a bluff with just the opposite intent. One might wish to be caught bluffing in order to be paid off with a good hand later because the opposition (wrongly)  suspects another bluff.
Bluff
To push is to go all-in.
All-In
In Hi/Lo poker variants, the qualifier refers to the minimum standard that a hand must meet to qualify for a particular share of the pot.
body.paragraph2: The other meaning of qualifier is as a synonym for satellite, a tournament in which one stands to win a spot in another tournament.

body.paragraph1: Hi/Lo, Tournament, Satellite
Satellite

A player quarters if he wins one fourth of the pot. This situation happens often in Hi/Lo games like Omaha Hi/Lo, when two players have only Lo hands of equal strength, so that they each get half of the whole pot reserved for the Lo hand. They each get half of the half pot.
Hi/Lo, Split Pot

In games with board cards in a round that ends without a showdown, a rabbit hunt means looking at the cards that would have been dealt to the board. For instance, a player who folded might wish to know whether he would have made his draw had he continued playing.
body.paragraph2: In TV games, the camera that shows the board cards that would been dealt is called the rabbit cam.

body.paragraph1: Fox Hunt
Rags are worthless or bad cards. In Texas Hold'em, for example, a starting hand of an ace and a low card like a 4 would be denoted ace rag.
Hole Cards
Cards of different suits, or more generally, a board that does not have the potential for a flush

A raise is an action that a player can make when confronted by another player's bet. To raise means to betover the amount wagered by your opponent. The number and size of raisesper round may be bounded depending on the house rules. Usually, 3 or 4,or less often 5 raises are allowed per betting round. A raise that follows an opposing raise is called a reraise.

If a player raises and that puts him all-in, i.e. he bets his whole stack, but does not have enough money or chips to make the minimum raise, then his raise is called incomplete or irregular. Depending on the house rules, such a raise might only be called incomplete if it is less than half of the minimum raise amount.
body.paragraph2: An incomplete raise does not reopen the betting, i.e. players who have already acted in the betting round cannot raise again. They can only call the incomplete raise or fold.

body.paragraph1: Player A bets $4.
body.paragraph2: Player B calls.
body.paragraph3: Player C has just $5 remaining and goes all-in
body.paragraph4: The minimum raise is $4. Player C must first pay $4 to see player A's bet and then has one remaining dollar with which to raise. His raise is incomplete since the smallest complete raise is $4. Players A and B can only increase their wagers by $1 or fold. They cannot raise again since they have already acted this round.

body.paragraph1: Check, Call, Bet, Fold, All-In, Complete
A raised pot, is a pot in which at least one player raised before the flop.
Unraised Pot, Multiway Pot

The rake or "chop" is the house's cut of each and every pot. A hand where a rake is taken is called a raked hand. The raked amount is subject to certain conditions. In general, a rake of 5% of the pot is taken, as long as the pot has exceeded a predetermined size. The maximum rake per hand limit is also determined by the particular establishment and by the limit being played. A cardroom will not take a percentage rake when a hand does not have a flop. This is called "no flop, no drop"
body.paragraph2: There are four rake types:

body.paragraph1: The rake collected at the end of a hand will be taken equally from all players who have been dealt into the hand.
The rake collected at the end of a hand will be taken equally from all players who have invested money in the pot.
The rake collected at the end of a hand will be taken from all players who have invested money in the pot, but calculated proportionately by the amount of money invested.
The rake of a hand will be assigned in full (100%) to the winner of the hand.
Chop

Some poker rooms offer "rakeback deals" which means that players get afixed percentage of their paid rake and tournament fees back into theiraccounts. These payments usually occur on a weekly or monthly basis,depending on the poker rooms.

The range of a player is all the possible cards with which he would play in a given situation. If a player in Texas Hold'em raises only AA, KK, QQ, and AK, for instance, then his range is: (AA,KK,QQ,AK), also denoted (QQ+, AK).

In the game, this translates to a question: with which hands would thisplayer act as he has in this situation? It might not matter, forinstance, whether the player has 55,66,77 or 88. If he plays all thesehands the same, then it is enough to know that he has a middle pocket pair.
body.paragraph2: The equity of one's own hand comes from the chances it has to win against the range of the opposition. An opponent might hold a stronger or weaker hand from his range, but as long as the range is well-estimated and the play against his range is correct, then it doesn't matter which particular hand from his range he has.

body.paragraph1: The range of a player always depends on the situation, the player, the opposing action, and the player's actions.
Hands with which a player would call a bet
Hands with which a player would raise a bet.
Hands with which a player would  raise first-in.
Hands with which a player would go all-in.

The level of card strength.
If a player has a made hand and additional draw to a better hand, then that draw is called a redraw.

body.paragraph2: Flop: 9 Diamonds, K Clubs, Q Diamonds
body.paragraph3: The player has a straight on a flop and the best hand. He also has a redraw to a flush in diamonds.
